High-level design interview prep HLD

Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Case studies and interview drills → Case study: social graph service

Lesson 3 of 9

Case study: social graph service

A social graph stores user relationships, supports high-volume fan-out queries, and powers many downstream ranking and recommendation systems.

25-35 min HLD flow Case studies and interview drills 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This is a good drill for graph-shaped data, adjacency lists, caching, and consistency semantics around relationship changes.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Model relationships clearly Adjacency lists often scale better than general graph traversals on the hot path2. Query paths Precompute counts and hot sets when useful3. Downstream consumers Publish edge changes to downstream systems

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This is a good drill for graph-shaped data, adjacency lists, caching, and consistency semantics around relationship changes. 2. Model relationships clearly Adjacency lists often scale better than general graph traversals on the hot path3. Query paths Precompute counts and hot sets when useful4. Downstream consumers Publish edge changes to downstream systems
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This is a good drill for graph-shaped data, adjacency lists, caching, and consistency semantics around relationship changes.

What to say first

  • Choose a graph representation based on hot queries.
  • Explain celebrity skew and cache strategy.
  • Differentiate edge types and their semantics.
2

Study section

Model relationships clearly

Follow, friend, mute, and block edges have different semantics and query patterns. Keep them explicit in the model.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Adjacency lists often scale better than general graph traversals on the hot path
  • Bidirectional friendship needs consistent update semantics
  • Relationship history may matter for audit or recommendation signals
3

Study section

Query paths

Common reads include followers, following, mutuals, and authorization checks such as block or visibility rules.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Precompute counts and hot sets when useful
  • Cache frequently accessed adjacency segments
  • Expect celebrity accounts to skew follower queries
4

Study section

Downstream consumers

Feeds, notifications, recommendations, and privacy checks often consume graph changes asynchronously.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Publish edge changes to downstream systems
  • Backfill derived views after repair or migration
  • Track lag between the graph and downstream indexes

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Graph-shaped domains often rely on adjacency patterns and derived downstream views.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Choose a graph representation based on hot queries.
  • Explain celebrity skew and cache strategy.
  • Differentiate edge types and their semantics.
  • Mention downstream consumers of relationship changes.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Using expensive graph traversals for every user-facing request.
  • Treating all relationship types as the same edge.
  • Ignoring the effect of graph changes on feeds and notifications.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you model follows versus mutual friendships?
  • What happens when a celebrity gains millions of followers?
  • Why might a graph database not be necessary for the hot path?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Feeds and timelines Common product design patternsThe NoSQL landscape Data storage and indexing

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: social graph service

Take the product shape from the case study and test one read path plus one write or ingestion path under pressure. Apply it to case study: social graph service so the trade-offs feel concrete.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Interview opening
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Interview opening Frame the problem, scope, assumptions, and success metrics before you draw components.

Interview opening

You are starting a mock interview for "Case study: social graph service". Capture the product assumptions, scale estimates, core APIs, and the first diagram you would draw.

Goal

Frame the problem, scope, assumptions, and success metrics before you draw components.

Guardrails

  • How would you model follows versus mutual friendships?
  • Model relationships clearly
  • Query paths
  • Choose a graph representation based on hot queries.

Suggested Structure

  • Requirements and assumptions
  • Scale estimate
  • Core APIs
  • First diagram

Bring your own model

Practice answer copilot

Direct browser call to the Chat Completions API.

OpenAI

Solution reveal

Compare your draft only when you are ready

These notes extend the original primer solution with a fuller interview walkthrough, a sample answer outline, and code you can discuss if the interviewer asks for implementation detail.
